chapters.title: Tiny Gains Compound Over Time

summaryBullets: This chapter argues that small repeated improvements create outsized change because results compound slowly before they become visible.
Clear presents habit change as a system problem more than a motivation problem.
A key idea is that compounding matters because repeated actions shape long term outcomes.
The chapter shows how tiny changes influences whether a behavior gets repeated.
It emphasizes that small actions are meaningful when they are easy to repeat consistently.
Readers are encouraged to reduce friction, increase clarity, and design behavior instead of depending on mood.
The lesson applies to personal routines, schoolwork, health, and work because habits govern everyday performance.
Its core message is that lasting change comes from shaping systems that make the right action more likely.
This chapter argues that small repeated improvements create outsized change because results compound slowly before they become visible.
Clear frames the issue through behavior design rather than through self blame.
A central point is that compounding works best when it is paired with clarity, repetition, and low friction.
The chapter explains how tiny changes can quietly strengthen or weaken the probability of follow through.
It challenges the belief that major change requires major effort in every moment.
Instead, the chapter shows that consistent tiny actions often outperform dramatic but unstable effort.
This means habit formation is partly about engineering the environment and the sequence of actions around you.
The chapter also suggests that identity, reward, and context influence whether behavior feels natural or forced.
For students and early career readers, the principle matters because performance is often the product of default systems.
The practical challenge is to create a habit that survives low energy days instead of one that only works in ideal conditions.
That requires making the start obvious, attractive, easy, or satisfying depending on where the system is weak.
The deeper lesson is that reliable progress comes from behavior design that compounds over time.

This chapter argues that small repeated improvements create outsized change because results compound slowly before they become visible, and it treats that claim as part of a broader system of behavior design.
Clear rejects the idea that people fail mainly because they lack character or motivation, and instead locates failure in unstable systems and poor design.
A major idea is that compounding matters because repeated behavior becomes identity evidence, environmental momentum, and eventually automatic routine.
The chapter also shows that tiny changes is not isolated from the rest of the system but interacts with cues, rewards, friction, and interpretation.
This matters because people often try to fix habits at the level of intention while ignoring the structure that makes one action likely and another difficult.
The chapter therefore treats small improvements as strategic, not trivial, because what repeats has more long term weight than what feels impressive once.
It also implies that the true challenge of habit change is designing for the ordinary day rather than for the exceptional day.
That principle protects people from the perfection trap, where a habit is defined so aggressively that it breaks as soon as life becomes messy.
Clear's method is practical because it ties psychology to design questions such as what triggers the action, how much friction exists, and what reward closes the loop.
For students and people in their twenties, this means habits around sleep, study, money, fitness, and work can be improved through systems rather than through recurring guilt.
The chapter also has an ethical layer, because better systems reduce the need for self punishment and increase the likelihood of honest self improvement.
It reframes discipline as an outcome of alignment rather than as constant inner conflict.
Another insight is that identity based change works best when people focus on repeated proof rather than on dramatic declarations about who they want to become.
The chapter further suggests that motivation is unstable, so durable progress comes from designing behavior that is still possible when enthusiasm drops.
The practical implication is to simplify, cue, reward, or constrain the environment until the desired behavior becomes easier to repeat than to avoid.
The deepest lesson is that remarkable change is usually the slow visible result of systems that quietly make good behavior more probable every day.

examples.title: Rebuilding a morning habit
examples.scenario: You want to journal, stretch, or read in the morning, but the habit disappears whenever your energy drops or your schedule changes. You keep trying to restart with a perfect plan and then feel discouraged when the system collapses after a few days.
whatToDo: Reduce the habit to a version you can do even on a messy day, place the cue where you will naturally see it, and treat consistency as the early goal instead of intensity. Once the habit is stable, add difficulty gradually instead of chasing motivation.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many personal habits fail from oversized expectations rather than from lack of desire. The chapter becomes practical when readers learn how to keep a habit alive through imperfect weeks.

examples.title: Changing how you see yourself
examples.scenario: You say you want to become healthier, more disciplined, or more focused, but your daily choices still reflect an old identity. Each missed habit feels like proof that you are still the same person you were before.
whatToDo: Shift the focus from outcomes to identity evidence. Ask what a disciplined person would do today in a very small way, then collect repeated proof through action instead of waiting to feel transformed first.
examples.whyItMatters: Identity based change is one of the most durable parts of the book. Readers need to experience it as a practical lens for daily decisions, not just as a motivational phrase.

examples.title: Building a study routine that survives stress
examples.scenario: You know you should study regularly, but you often wait for long blocks of perfect focus that never fully arrive. When the semester gets busy, the habit breaks and the workload becomes a crisis instead of a routine.
whatToDo: Create a cue tied to an existing behavior, define a tiny opening version of the study habit, and make the first few minutes frictionless. Use visible tracking so the real reward becomes consistency rather than last minute panic fueled effort.
examples.whyItMatters: Students often fail from irregular systems more than from low ability. This example helps them apply habit design to schoolwork in a way that compounds across the semester.

examples.title: Using social norms to improve performance
examples.scenario: Your habits shift depending on the people around you. In one environment you procrastinate, drift, and avoid hard work, while in another you naturally focus because the culture makes disciplined behavior feel normal.
whatToDo: Move closer to people and environments where the habits you want are already standard. Join a study group, library routine, or accountability system that makes focus socially reinforced instead of socially unusual.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because students underestimate how much their surrounding culture shapes behavior. The chapter becomes real when they treat the environment as part of the habit itself.

examples.title: Designing better work defaults
examples.scenario: At work you want to focus on high value tasks, but your environment keeps inviting distraction through open tabs, unclear next steps, notifications, and reactive requests. Good intentions are not enough because the default setup is working against you.
whatToDo: Reduce friction for the work that matters most by preparing the workspace in advance, defining the next concrete action, and making distractions less available during focus blocks. Build the environment so the easier move supports the right behavior.
examples.whyItMatters: This example translates the book from self improvement language into operational design. Better work often comes from better defaults, not from repeated acts of heroic willpower.

examples.title: Avoiding the plateau trap
examples.scenario: You improve for a while in a skill or routine, then the progress seems to level off. Because the gains are less dramatic, you get bored, lose attention, and start switching systems instead of refining the one that already works.
whatToDo: Expect the plateau as part of the process and keep the core habit alive while adding measured challenge. Use reflection to spot where repetition has become automatic and where a small increase in difficulty would restore engagement.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many people abandon good systems during the slow middle. The chapter becomes useful when readers understand how to survive the phase where compounding is real but not yet exciting.

whatToDo: Reduce the habit to a version you can do even on a messy day, place the cue where you will naturally see it, and treat consistency as the early goal instead of intensity. Once the habit is stable, add difficulty gradually instead of chasing motivation.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many personal habits fail from oversized expectations rather than from lack of desire. The chapter becomes practical when readers learn how to keep a habit alive through imperfect weeks.

examples.title: Changing how you see yourself
examples.scenario: You say you want to become healthier, more disciplined, or more focused, but your daily choices still reflect an old identity. Each missed habit feels like proof that you are still the same person you were before.
whatToDo: Shift the focus from outcomes to identity evidence. Ask what a disciplined person would do today in a very small way, then collect repeated proof through action instead of waiting to feel transformed first.
examples.whyItMatters: Identity based change is one of the most durable parts of the book. Readers need to experience it as a practical lens for daily decisions, not just as a motivational phrase.

examples.title: Building a study routine that survives stress
examples.scenario: You know you should study regularly, but you often wait for long blocks of perfect focus that never fully arrive. When the semester gets busy, the habit breaks and the workload becomes a crisis instead of a routine.
whatToDo: Create a cue tied to an existing behavior, define a tiny opening version of the study habit, and make the first few minutes frictionless. Use visible tracking so the real reward becomes consistency rather than last minute panic fueled effort.
examples.whyItMatters: Students often fail from irregular systems more than from low ability. This example helps them apply habit design to schoolwork in a way that compounds across the semester.

examples.title: Using social norms to improve performance
examples.scenario: Your habits shift depending on the people around you. In one environment you procrastinate, drift, and avoid hard work, while in another you naturally focus because the culture makes disciplined behavior feel normal.
whatToDo: Move closer to people and environments where the habits you want are already standard. Join a study group, library routine, or accountability system that makes focus socially reinforced instead of socially unusual.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because students underestimate how much their surrounding culture shapes behavior. The chapter becomes real when they treat the environment as part of the habit itself.

examples.title: Designing better work defaults
examples.scenario: At work you want to focus on high value tasks, but your environment keeps inviting distraction through open tabs, unclear next steps, notifications, and reactive requests. Good intentions are not enough because the default setup is working against you.
whatToDo: Reduce friction for the work that matters most by preparing the workspace in advance, defining the next concrete action, and making distractions less available during focus blocks. Build the environment so the easier move supports the right behavior.
examples.whyItMatters: This example translates the book from self improvement language into operational design. Better work often comes from better defaults, not from repeated acts of heroic willpower.

examples.title: Avoiding the plateau trap
examples.scenario: You improve for a while in a skill or routine, then the progress seems to level off. Because the gains are less dramatic, you get bored, lose attention, and start switching systems instead of refining the one that already works.
whatToDo: Expect the plateau as part of the process and keep the core habit alive while adding measured challenge. Use reflection to spot where repetition has become automatic and where a small increase in difficulty would restore engagement.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many people abandon good systems during the slow middle. The chapter becomes useful when readers understand how to survive the phase where compounding is real but not yet exciting.

chapters.title: Plan The Exact Moment To Act

summaryBullets: This chapter argues that clarity about when and where you will act increases follow through.
Clear presents habit change as a system problem more than a motivation problem.
A key idea is that implementation intention matters because repeated actions shape long term outcomes.
The chapter shows how clarity influences whether a behavior gets repeated.

This chapter argues that clarity about when and where you will act increases follow through.
Clear frames the issue through behavior design rather than through self blame.
A central point is that implementation intention works best when it is paired with clarity, repetition, and low friction.
The chapter explains how clarity can quietly strengthen or weaken the probability of follow through.

The chapter also suggests that identity, reward, and context influence whether behavior feels natural or forced.
For students and early career readers, the principle matters because performance is often the product of default systems.

This chapter argues that clarity about when and where you will act increases follow through, and it treats that claim as part of a broader system of behavior design.
Clear rejects the idea that people fail mainly because they lack character or motivation, and instead locates failure in unstable systems and poor design.
A major idea is that implementation intention matters because repeated behavior becomes identity evidence, environmental momentum, and eventually automatic routine.
The chapter also shows that clarity is not isolated from the rest of the system but interacts with cues, rewards, friction, and interpretation.
This matters because people often try to fix habits at the level of intention while ignoring the structure that makes one action likely and another difficult.
The chapter therefore treats small improvements as strategic, not trivial, because what repeats has more long term weight than what feels impressive once.
It also implies that the true challenge of habit change is designing for the ordinary day rather than for the exceptional day.
That principle protects people from the perfection trap, where a habit is defined so aggressively that it breaks as soon as life becomes messy.
Clear's method is practical because it ties psychology to design questions such as what triggers the action, how much friction exists, and what reward closes the loop.

The chapter also has an ethical layer, because better systems reduce the need for self punishment and increase the likelihood of honest self improvement.

Another insight is that identity based change works best when people focus on repeated proof rather than on dramatic declarations about who they want to become.

The practical implication is to simplify, cue, reward, or constrain the environment until the desired behavior becomes easier to repeat than to avoid.

examples.title: Rebuilding a morning habit
examples.scenario: You want to journal, stretch, or read in the morning, but the habit disappears whenever your energy drops or your schedule changes. You keep trying to restart with a perfect plan and then feel discouraged when the system collapses after a few days.
whatToDo: Reduce the habit to a version you can do even on a messy day, place the cue where you will naturally see it, and treat consistency as the early goal instead of intensity. Once the habit is stable, add difficulty gradually instead of chasing motivation.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many personal habits fail from oversized expectations rather than from lack of desire. The chapter becomes practical when readers learn how to keep a habit alive through imperfect weeks.

examples.title: Changing how you see yourself
examples.scenario: You say you want to become healthier, more disciplined, or more focused, but your daily choices still reflect an old identity. Each missed habit feels like proof that you are still the same person you were before.
whatToDo: Shift the focus from outcomes to identity evidence. Ask what a disciplined person would do today in a very small way, then collect repeated proof through action instead of waiting to feel transformed first.
examples.whyItMatters: Identity based change is one of the most durable parts of the book. Readers need to experience it as a practical lens for daily decisions, not just as a motivational phrase.

examples.title: Building a study routine that survives stress
examples.scenario: You know you should study regularly, but you often wait for long blocks of perfect focus that never fully arrive. When the semester gets busy, the habit breaks and the workload becomes a crisis instead of a routine.
whatToDo: Create a cue tied to an existing behavior, define a tiny opening version of the study habit, and make the first few minutes frictionless. Use visible tracking so the real reward becomes consistency rather than last minute panic fueled effort.
examples.whyItMatters: Students often fail from irregular systems more than from low ability. This example helps them apply habit design to schoolwork in a way that compounds across the semester.

examples.title: Using social norms to improve performance
examples.scenario: Your habits shift depending on the people around you. In one environment you procrastinate, drift, and avoid hard work, while in another you naturally focus because the culture makes disciplined behavior feel normal.
whatToDo: Move closer to people and environments where the habits you want are already standard. Join a study group, library routine, or accountability system that makes focus socially reinforced instead of socially unusual.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because students underestimate how much their surrounding culture shapes behavior. The chapter becomes real when they treat the environment as part of the habit itself.

examples.title: Designing better work defaults
examples.scenario: At work you want to focus on high value tasks, but your environment keeps inviting distraction through open tabs, unclear next steps, notifications, and reactive requests. Good intentions are not enough because the default setup is working against you.
whatToDo: Reduce friction for the work that matters most by preparing the workspace in advance, defining the next concrete action, and making distractions less available during focus blocks. Build the environment so the easier move supports the right behavior.
examples.whyItMatters: This example translates the book from self improvement language into operational design. Better work often comes from better defaults, not from repeated acts of heroic willpower.

examples.title: Avoiding the plateau trap
examples.scenario: You improve for a while in a skill or routine, then the progress seems to level off. Because the gains are less dramatic, you get bored, lose attention, and start switching systems instead of refining the one that already works.
whatToDo: Expect the plateau as part of the process and keep the core habit alive while adding measured challenge. Use reflection to spot where repetition has become automatic and where a small increase in difficulty would restore engagement.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many people abandon good systems during the slow middle. The chapter becomes useful when readers understand how to survive the phase where compounding is real but not yet exciting.

examples.title: Rebuilding a morning habit
examples.scenario: You want to journal, stretch, or read in the morning, but the habit disappears whenever your energy drops or your schedule changes. You keep trying to restart with a perfect plan and then feel discouraged when the system collapses after a few days.
whatToDo: Reduce the habit to a version you can do even on a messy day, place the cue where you will naturally see it, and treat consistency as the early goal instead of intensity. Once the habit is stable, add difficulty gradually instead of chasing motivation.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many personal habits fail from oversized expectations rather than from lack of desire. The chapter becomes practical when readers learn how to keep a habit alive through imperfect weeks.

examples.title: Changing how you see yourself
examples.scenario: You say you want to become healthier, more disciplined, or more focused, but your daily choices still reflect an old identity. Each missed habit feels like proof that you are still the same person you were before.
whatToDo: Shift the focus from outcomes to identity evidence. Ask what a disciplined person would do today in a very small way, then collect repeated proof through action instead of waiting to feel transformed first.
examples.whyItMatters: Identity based change is one of the most durable parts of the book. Readers need to experience it as a practical lens for daily decisions, not just as a motivational phrase.

examples.title: Building a study routine that survives stress
examples.scenario: You know you should study regularly, but you often wait for long blocks of perfect focus that never fully arrive. When the semester gets busy, the habit breaks and the workload becomes a crisis instead of a routine.
whatToDo: Create a cue tied to an existing behavior, define a tiny opening version of the study habit, and make the first few minutes frictionless. Use visible tracking so the real reward becomes consistency rather than last minute panic fueled effort.
examples.whyItMatters: Students often fail from irregular systems more than from low ability. This example helps them apply habit design to schoolwork in a way that compounds across the semester.

examples.title: Using social norms to improve performance
examples.scenario: Your habits shift depending on the people around you. In one environment you procrastinate, drift, and avoid hard work, while in another you naturally focus because the culture makes disciplined behavior feel normal.
whatToDo: Move closer to people and environments where the habits you want are already standard. Join a study group, library routine, or accountability system that makes focus socially reinforced instead of socially unusual.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because students underestimate how much their surrounding culture shapes behavior. The chapter becomes real when they treat the environment as part of the habit itself.

examples.title: Designing better work defaults
examples.scenario: At work you want to focus on high value tasks, but your environment keeps inviting distraction through open tabs, unclear next steps, notifications, and reactive requests. Good intentions are not enough because the default setup is working against you.
whatToDo: Reduce friction for the work that matters most by preparing the workspace in advance, defining the next concrete action, and making distractions less available during focus blocks. Build the environment so the easier move supports the right behavior.
examples.whyItMatters: This example translates the book from self improvement language into operational design. Better work often comes from better defaults, not from repeated acts of heroic willpower.

examples.title: Avoiding the plateau trap
examples.scenario: You improve for a while in a skill or routine, then the progress seems to level off. Because the gains are less dramatic, you get bored, lose attention, and start switching systems instead of refining the one that already works.
whatToDo: Expect the plateau as part of the process and keep the core habit alive while adding measured challenge. Use reflection to spot where repetition has become automatic and where a small increase in difficulty would restore engagement.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many people abandon good systems during the slow middle. The chapter becomes useful when readers understand how to survive the phase where compounding is real but not yet exciting.

examples.title: Designing better work defaults
examples.scenario: At work you want to focus on high value tasks, but your environment keeps inviting distraction through open tabs, unclear next steps, notifications, and reactive requests. Good intentions are not enough because the default setup is working against you.

examples.title: Avoiding the plateau trap

examples.title: Rebuilding a morning habit

examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many personal habits fail from oversized expectations rather than from lack of desire. The chapter becomes practical when readers learn how to keep a habit alive through imperfect weeks.

examples.title: Changing how you see yourself
examples.scenario: You say you want to become healthier, more disciplined, or more focused, but your daily choices still reflect an old identity. Each missed habit feels like proof that you are still the same person you were before.
whatToDo: Shift the focus from outcomes to identity evidence. Ask what a disciplined person would do today in a very small way, then collect repeated proof through action instead of waiting to feel transformed first.
examples.whyItMatters: Identity based change is one of the most durable parts of the book. Readers need to experience it as a practical lens for daily decisions, not just as a motivational phrase.

examples.title: Building a study routine that survives stress
examples.scenario: You know you should study regularly, but you often wait for long blocks of perfect focus that never fully arrive. When the semester gets busy, the habit breaks and the workload becomes a crisis instead of a routine.

chapters.title: Habits Work Better When They Fit You

summaryBullets: This chapter argues that results improve when habits match natural inclinations and context.
Clear presents habit change as a system problem more than a motivation problem.
A key idea is that natural fit matters because repeated actions shape long term outcomes.
The chapter shows how strength alignment influences whether a behavior gets repeated.
It emphasizes that small actions are meaningful when they are easy to repeat consistently.
Readers are encouraged to reduce friction, increase clarity, and design behavior instead of depending on mood.
The lesson applies to personal routines, schoolwork, health, and work because habits govern everyday performance.
Its core message is that lasting change comes from shaping systems that make the right action more likely.
This chapter argues that results improve when habits match natural inclinations and context.
Clear frames the issue through behavior design rather than through self blame.
A central point is that natural fit works best when it is paired with clarity, repetition, and low friction.
The chapter explains how strength alignment can quietly strengthen or weaken the probability of follow through.
It challenges the belief that major change requires major effort in every moment.

The chapter also suggests that identity, reward, and context influence whether behavior feels natural or forced.
For students and early career readers, the principle matters because performance is often the product of default systems.
The practical challenge is to create a habit that survives low energy days instead of one that only works in ideal conditions.

The deeper lesson is that reliable progress comes from behavior design that compounds over time.
This chapter argues that results improve when habits match natural inclinations and context, and it treats that claim as part of a broader system of behavior design.
Clear rejects the idea that people fail mainly because they lack character or motivation, and instead locates failure in unstable systems and poor design.
A major idea is that natural fit matters because repeated behavior becomes identity evidence, environmental momentum, and eventually automatic routine.
The chapter also shows that strength alignment is not isolated from the rest of the system but interacts with cues, rewards, friction, and interpretation.
This matters because people often try to fix habits at the level of intention while ignoring the structure that makes one action likely and another difficult.
The chapter therefore treats small improvements as strategic, not trivial, because what repeats has more long term weight than what feels impressive once.
It also implies that the true challenge of habit change is designing for the ordinary day rather than for the exceptional day.
That principle protects people from the perfection trap, where a habit is defined so aggressively that it breaks as soon as life becomes messy.
Clear's method is practical because it ties psychology to design questions such as what triggers the action, how much friction exists, and what reward closes the loop.

The chapter also has an ethical layer, because better systems reduce the need for self punishment and increase the likelihood of honest self improvement.
It reframes discipline as an outcome of alignment rather than as constant inner conflict.

The chapter further suggests that motivation is unstable, so durable progress comes from designing behavior that is still possible when enthusiasm drops.
The practical implication is to simplify, cue, reward, or constrain the environment until the desired behavior becomes easier to repeat than to avoid.

examples.title: Rebuilding a morning habit
examples.scenario: You want to journal, stretch, or read in the morning, but the habit disappears whenever your energy drops or your schedule changes. You keep trying to restart with a perfect plan and then feel discouraged when the system collapses after a few days.
whatToDo: Reduce the habit to a version you can do even on a messy day, place the cue where you will naturally see it, and treat consistency as the early goal instead of intensity. Once the habit is stable, add difficulty gradually instead of chasing motivation.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many personal habits fail from oversized expectations rather than from lack of desire. The chapter becomes practical when readers learn how to keep a habit alive through imperfect weeks.

examples.title: Changing how you see yourself
examples.scenario: You say you want to become healthier, more disciplined, or more focused, but your daily choices still reflect an old identity. Each missed habit feels like proof that you are still the same person you were before.
whatToDo: Shift the focus from outcomes to identity evidence. Ask what a disciplined person would do today in a very small way, then collect repeated proof through action instead of waiting to feel transformed first.
examples.whyItMatters: Identity based change is one of the most durable parts of the book. Readers need to experience it as a practical lens for daily decisions, not just as a motivational phrase.

examples.title: Building a study routine that survives stress
examples.scenario: You know you should study regularly, but you often wait for long blocks of perfect focus that never fully arrive. When the semester gets busy, the habit breaks and the workload becomes a crisis instead of a routine.
whatToDo: Create a cue tied to an existing behavior, define a tiny opening version of the study habit, and make the first few minutes frictionless. Use visible tracking so the real reward becomes consistency rather than last minute panic fueled effort.
examples.whyItMatters: Students often fail from irregular systems more than from low ability. This example helps them apply habit design to schoolwork in a way that compounds across the semester.

examples.title: Using social norms to improve performance
examples.scenario: Your habits shift depending on the people around you. In one environment you procrastinate, drift, and avoid hard work, while in another you naturally focus because the culture makes disciplined behavior feel normal.
whatToDo: Move closer to people and environments where the habits you want are already standard. Join a study group, library routine, or accountability system that makes focus socially reinforced instead of socially unusual.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because students underestimate how much their surrounding culture shapes behavior. The chapter becomes real when they treat the environment as part of the habit itself.

examples.title: Designing better work defaults
examples.scenario: At work you want to focus on high value tasks, but your environment keeps inviting distraction through open tabs, unclear next steps, notifications, and reactive requests. Good intentions are not enough because the default setup is working against you.
whatToDo: Reduce friction for the work that matters most by preparing the workspace in advance, defining the next concrete action, and making distractions less available during focus blocks. Build the environment so the easier move supports the right behavior.
examples.whyItMatters: This example translates the book from self improvement language into operational design. Better work often comes from better defaults, not from repeated acts of heroic willpower.

examples.title: Avoiding the plateau trap
examples.scenario: You improve for a while in a skill or routine, then the progress seems to level off. Because the gains are less dramatic, you get bored, lose attention, and start switching systems instead of refining the one that already works.
whatToDo: Expect the plateau as part of the process and keep the core habit alive while adding measured challenge. Use reflection to spot where repetition has become automatic and where a small increase in difficulty would restore engagement.
examples.whyItMatters: This matters because many people abandon good systems during the slow middle. The chapter becomes useful when readers understand how to survive the phase where compounding is real but not yet exciting.
